title: Dear Life Kit: I’m sick of all this unwanted attention
intro: Clinical psychologist Adia Gooden and NPR's It's Been A Minute host, Brittany Luse, answer questions from Dear Life Kit listeners and they share their best advice on unsolicited advice.Follow us on Instagram: @nprlifekitSign up for our newsletter here.Have an episode idea or feedback you want to share? Email us at lifekit@npr.orgSupport the show and listen to it sponsor-free by signing up for Life Kit+ at plus.npr.org/lifekit

transcript.author: MARIELLE SEGARRA, HOST
transcript.text: You're listening to LIFE KIT...

transcript.author: SEGARRA
transcript.text: ...From NPR.

transcript.author: SEGARRA
transcript.text: Hey. It's Marielle. Today, we're talking about how to deal with unwanted and unwarranted advice. Now, what's that old expression? Opinions are like adrenal glands - most of us have them, and we tend to think ours are the best. Which is to say, a lot of people have strong opinions about offering advice and opinions.

transcript.author: BRITTANY LUSE, BYLINE
transcript.text: Oh, I love giving advice - unfortunately, unsolicited (laughter).

transcript.author: SEGARRA
transcript.text: See what I mean? That was NPR's Brittany Luse, by the way. If you don't know her already, she's the host of It's Been A Minute, and every week, she does a deep dive into what's going on in our culture and helps listeners make sense of it. So it makes sense that she would have hot takes on things, and I would trust them.

transcript.author: LUSE
transcript.text: But I do seek advice. I'm comfortable with not being an expert on something and not knowing something and going to somebody, like, to be like, can you help me with this, or what do you think I should do?

transcript.author: SEGARRA
transcript.text: The thing is, there is a difference between someone attempting to be helpful and just straight-up judging or criticizing you.

transcript.author: ADIA GOODEN
transcript.text: I'm someone who's like, if you have wisdom, like, I'll learn from it. I'm happy to learn from it. But I do think it's different if you're like, no, I'm going along just fine, and I don't need your opinion that might be judgmental or, like, you're doing it wrong. Like, that advice is generally, like, nobody likes it.

transcript.author: SEGARRA
transcript.text: That's clinical psychologist Adia Gooden. You might recognize her from our episodes on how to be a good loser or how to stop hurting your own feelings. She's all about embracing unconditional self-worth, a very useful skill to have in your back pocket when it comes to today's topic. On this edition of Dear LIFE KIT, the advice series from LIFE KIT reporter Andee Tagle, Brittany and Adia are going to give you advice about advice. They'll talk you through how to handle hurtful in-laws, overly curious onlookers and those family members that mean well but can't seem to quit with their lofty expectations.

transcript.author: ANDEE TAGLE, BYLINE
transcript.text: OK, everybody, here's Question 1.

transcript.author: ANDEE TAGLE, BYLINE
transcript.text: (Reading) Dear LIFE KIT, I'm struggling with my relationship with my future sister-in-law. She often says things that are hurtful or judgmental. One time, she told me, I love you, but you're too much of a self-absorbed person to own a dog. And this was over a meal that I had brought over for her. I've had pets in the past, raised my younger sister, taking care of my sick mom. I volunteer regularly. I don't live my life in a way that I think most would consider self-absorbed. She frequently comments on the decisions my fiancé, her brother and I make. Her comments make us second-guess our decisions and bring us down. In the past, when my fiancé has brought up an opposing opinion, she shuts him down and is adamant that she is right and he is wrong. So lately, I've begun avoiding her. How can I cope with the situation in a healthy way and have a healthy relationship with her? Signed, Feeling Frozen.

transcript.author: GOODEN
transcript.text: Well, I - you know, I think this is a situation of needing to recognize when someone's comments are not about you. I mean, many of the times when we interact with people, it's not - it's about them. It's not about us. And if you know for yourself that you are not selfish, that you are not self-absorbed, then you shouldn't let somebody's comment that you are derail you or override what you know to be true from your own experience, from how you've shown up. So I think the first thing is, how do you remember who you are and know who you are and be grounded in that? And then how do you determine and discern whose opinion you're going to take and whose opinion you're going to leave, right?

transcript.author: GOODEN
transcript.text: Like, if it's your wise grandma and she says, hey, it seems like you've been focusing on yourself a little too much and I want you to think about this, that might be very different than a sister-in-law who you don't really feel knows you, tends to make judgmental comments and is often making you feel bad, right? So she may be somebody who you're like, I'm going to leave your opinions over there because that's about you. It's really not about me. And then you may also need to set some boundaries. Like, hey, you know, it feels - I feel judged when you give me advice, so I'd prefer that not to be part of our relationship - or something, right? You may have a conversation at some point.

transcript.author: LUSE
transcript.text: I echo that. I think that you can't control her. You can only control how you...

transcript.author: TAGLE
transcript.text: Right

transcript.author: LUSE
transcript.text: ...How and when you interact with her to a certain degree. And also, like, how you want to take on what she's saying to and about you. Another question that I have, though - where is your fiancé in all this? Like, at the end of the day...

transcript.author: TAGLE
transcript.text: Yeah, that was my question, too.

transcript.author: LUSE
transcript.text: ...That's his sister. And I think he could play maybe a bigger role in managing the - like, your relationship as a couple to her. It also sounds like maybe there's something going on where he's also taking on a lot as far as, like, her judgments of him and your guys' relationship. And that is also something to kind of, like, be aware of. Like, I remember my therapist at one point saying to me when I was talking about somebody that I was having a difficult issue with, and she said, you know, have you ever considered that this person may never change? Like, I want you to consider that...

transcript.author: TAGLE
transcript.text: Oof.

transcript.author: LUSE
transcript.text: ...And then think about how you want to conduct yourself going forward. And, yeah, I think that's not just something for you to do, but it sounds like it may be something for your fiancé to do as well.

transcript.author: TAGLE
transcript.text: Yeah. The one question that I had here is, as they're starting a new life, as they're starting to pull away from the sister, like, the optics of that can feel hard.

transcript.author: GOODEN
transcript.text: It seems like there might be space for them to distance without, like, totally cutting off. And I think if people question, you could also just - you can give people an opportunity to do something different. You can also say, hey, you know, every time you make these comments, it makes me feel judged and criticized, and I don't like it, and I'm hoping you could stop. And you can give them an opportunity to stop, and they can try. And if they do, great. And if they don't, they'll know exactly why there has been a shift.

transcript.author: LUSE
transcript.text: Yeah.

transcript.author: TAGLE
transcript.text: Wow.

transcript.author: LUSE
transcript.text: That makes me think of something that I say (laughter) as, like, a personal philosophy, which is, like, I'm going to give as much as I can lovingly and no more. 'Cause if you give more than what you can give lovingly, you're either going to - either leads to conflict, or it leads to you feeling shortchanged. Even if the other person is fine - right? - you're feeling terrible 'cause you feel like you overextended yourself and you got trampled on. And it just - it doesn't make anybody happy.

transcript.author: TAGLE
transcript.text: Wow. I love that.

transcript.author: GOODEN
transcript.text: That's a great philosophy.

transcript.author: TAGLE
transcript.text: Moving on to Question 2.

transcript.author: TAGLE
transcript.text: (Reading) Dear LIFE KIT, as a 6-foot-tall woman, I am often told by complete strangers variations of, wow, you're tall. It makes me feel uncomfortable and a bit like a spectacle. Some people say I should take it as a compliment, but I think it's rude for others to announce my most prominent feature to me with a tone of shock. I don't want to normalize the comments, but I don't want to embarrass the commenters either. How can I respond? Signed, More Than My Height.

transcript.author: LUSE
transcript.text: More Than My Height, I appreciate the fact that you don't want to embarrass the people who are making these comments. But I just want to offer maybe not 100% embarrassment or even, like, 60% embarrassment, but maybe a nice little gritty 15% embarrassment where you can respond with, like, oh, my gosh, you are - you know, you're the first person to tell me that.

transcript.author: LUSE
transcript.text: And just give them a little giggle. And then you kind of give them the opportunity to laugh with you, but they know, like, you're not playing. And then if they don't take the hint the first time, then you can just be direct and be like, is this the thing that's catching your attention so much that you have to say something right now? I just feel like if you can give someone sort of, like, one pass with a little joke to sort of let them know that you don't like it, and then after that, I think if you're really direct, then people will get it.

transcript.author: GOODEN
transcript.text: Yeah. I agree with Brittany. If people are continuing to sort of, like, cross this boundary, being more serious about it and being like, it's not cool. I'm not comfortable with it. Please stop. And I think, you know, if somebody - I agree. If somebody feels a little embarrassed or a little like, ooh, I shouldn't have done - it's like, that's true, and that's OK. You're not saying, you're an awful person that you would ever do - right? You're not doing that. But you're communicating, like, actually, what you did is problematic. Somebody else should...

transcript.author: TAGLE
transcript.text: Yeah.

transcript.author: GOODEN
transcript.text: ...Feel a little taste of it so that they stop doing it.

transcript.author: TAGLE
transcript.text: Right.

transcript.author: LUSE
transcript.text: That's good.

transcript.author: TAGLE
transcript.text: Yeah.

transcript.author: TAGLE
transcript.text: More questions from Dear LIFE KIT after this.

transcript.author: TAGLE
transcript.text: Moving on to Question No. 3.

transcript.author: TAGLE
transcript.text: (Reading) Dear LIFE KIT, I've been with my boyfriend for seven years, and I'm starting to feel pressure from family members and friends to settle down and have a wedding. We're planning on getting married, but neither of us are financially stable right now. We want to wait until we are in a more secure place to make wedding plans. How can I let people know that we're not in a hurry to get married without being rude? Signed...

transcript.author: LUSE
transcript.text: I like this. Oh, go ahead. Sorry.

transcript.author: TAGLE
transcript.text: Yeah. Signed, Not In A Hurry, but also could be signed by everybody on Earth, for sure. We all have a version of this, I think. Single, partnered, married, not married, everyone loves to tell you that you are behind in your life. You are doing...

transcript.author: LUSE
transcript.text: Yeah.

transcript.author: TAGLE
transcript.text: ...It wrong. Brittany, thoughts?

transcript.author: LUSE
transcript.text: I lived this. When you literally say - like, my husband and I, we got - he proposed after three years, which for some people is way too long. And then by the time we got married in 2022, we had been together about seven years (laughter). And everybody was asking, like, when are you getting married? And then as soon as we did that - think you got everyone off your back, and then everyone's like, OK, well, where are your kids? And, you know, at the end of the day, I think there's, like, two things happening. One thing is that people in your life probably see that you're very happy and are happy for your relationship and want to celebrate it and you and would love to see you feel the happiness that perhaps they get or have gotten from those celebrations that they've had of their own. So it can be coming from, like, a very loving place. And then...

transcript.author: TAGLE
transcript.text: Yes.

transcript.author: LUSE
transcript.text: ...There's also, like, another aspect of it, which is that people, I think, also sometimes feel a lot of anxiety if they feel like you are not hitting certain milestones in a timeline that's comfortable for them. And then they can take that anxiety and project it onto you. And, I mean, as you are bringing your lives together to get married, thinking back to that first question we discussed, now is a great time for you all to discuss and strategize how you're going to deal with feedback and input from other people. And what I said to people maybe is probably not as great as what Adia might say you should say to people. But I said to people, if you got $30,000 for me that you want to break off, like, if you want to deposit that into my...

transcript.author: TAGLE
transcript.text: (Laughter).

transcript.author: LUSE
transcript.text: If you got tens of thousands of dollars that you want to give me, go right ahead. The other thing that I would say that was mildly gentler is, like, I don't have any updates to share right now, but when I do, you will be the first to know, which is what I say now when people ask me about having children. I say, oh, I don't have any updates right now. But when I do, you'll be the first to know.

transcript.author: TAGLE
transcript.text: That's so funny. That's exactly what I tell my parent when they ask about the second, when they ask about - so now my new benchmark is for a sibling.

transcript.author: LUSE
transcript.text: (Laughter).

transcript.author: TAGLE
transcript.text: And I'm like, OK, are you paying for day care, or when are you moving in? Yeah, there's never an end to the benchmark. This is all very good. Adia, what do you have for us?

transcript.author: GOODEN
transcript.text: Yeah. I agree with what Brittany said. I think if people are coming from a loving place, just saying, like, you know, we're being really intentional and thoughtful about how we want to start our next chapter of our lives together. Weddings are expensive. We don't want to start our lives together in debt or going into debt. I think sort of touching on what Brittany mentioned about anxiety - right? - sometimes, you know, it could be anxiety of, like, you know, if you don't marry him right away, he going to leave. And so I think, again, sort of, like, it might be a time when you're like - either like, you know, like, thanks for your opinion, but we're going to do what's right for us, or, like, letting it roll off and just being like, OK. Like, I'm glad you - you know, that's not our situation. Like, we're not - I'm not worried he's going to leave me. I'm not worried this or that, the other's going to happen. I think making a joke about it can be good, too, because people are like, oh, oh. Oh, OK. Right? They'll usually back off.

transcript.author: TAGLE
transcript.text: I like that. My last question for you is, we all have those people in our lives that push harder, you know? There's a spectrum. So when that light touch doesn't work, what's level two?

transcript.author: GOODEN
transcript.text: What Brittany was saying. It's like, you know, please stop asking us. It's kind of frustrating and annoying when you keep asking us about our plans. We will be sure to let you know when we are going in that direction. But until then, I just ask that you respect our decision-making.

transcript.author: LUSE
transcript.text: I agree. You just got to - you got to really push back. Or - I mean, I have never really had to do this, but, like, you could also - (laughter) like, if it gets to a certain point, you could just give them a complete, like, I already told you. I'm not discussing this. And then you just wait (laughter). Oh, my gosh. It unsettles people.

transcript.author: GOODEN
transcript.text: Yeah. 'Cause I think part of it is people want you to get into a negotiation with them.

transcript.author: TAGLE
transcript.text: Right.

transcript.author: GOODEN
transcript.text: Like, you feeling like, oh, I have to give all my reasons. And you, like what Brittany said, it's like, I already told you. It's like, I'm not engaging in this discussion. Like, our decision is made.

transcript.author: TAGLE
transcript.text: Right.

transcript.author: GOODEN
transcript.text: And because people want to be able to, I want to convince you, or why do you think? Well, no, our decision is made.

transcript.author: LUSE
transcript.text: Yep.

transcript.author: GOODEN
transcript.text: And then not engaging.

transcript.author: TAGLE
transcript.text: Not negotiating your life plan.

transcript.author: LUSE
transcript.text: So funny. I feel like so much of the advice we've given today is like, (laughter) don't take other people's advice.

transcript.author: TAGLE
transcript.text: Take our advice.

transcript.author: LUSE
transcript.text: But not others'.

transcript.author: TAGLE
transcript.text: (Laughter) OK, our final question - Question No. 4.

transcript.author: TAGLE
transcript.text: Dear LIFE KIT, I've grown close with a group of coworkers lately. One friend in our group will be getting married later this year. Most of us have mixed feelings about our friend's upcoming marriage. She seems happy, but we disapprove of how her fiancé treats her. We think she deserves better. We've observed him in group settings. He's disengaged, hard to talk to and acts as if he's too good for the gathering. We consider that he might just be shy or awkward, but another coworker has noticed that our friend's fiancé withholds affection and bosses her around. A few of us talked it over and agreed that if we were in her situation, we would want someone to tell us. Should we tell her? Signed, Caring Coworkers.

transcript.author: GOODEN
transcript.text: I do not think it's true that they would want to know from a coworker if their coworker did not like their fiancé.

transcript.author: TAGLE
transcript.text: Right. Yeah. In what world? In what world?

transcript.author: GOODEN
transcript.text: You would not.

transcript.author: LUSE
transcript.text: Ever.

transcript.author: GOODEN
transcript.text: You would not, right?

transcript.author: LUSE
transcript.text: No, no.

transcript.author: TAGLE
transcript.text: Like, from one, from all?

transcript.author: GOODEN
transcript.text: Right.

transcript.author: TAGLE
transcript.text: You got to pull them into a conference room and be like...

transcript.author: LUSE
transcript.text: She will cut you all off.

transcript.author: GOODEN
transcript.text: (Laughter) We've all had enough relationships with people we probably shouldn't have been with and seen friends in relationships with people that weren't great to know that it basically never works to be like, girl, he ain't for you.

transcript.author: TAGLE
transcript.text: Yeah. Yeah.

transcript.author: LUSE
transcript.text: Yeah.

transcript.author: GOODEN
transcript.text: When have we listened to that?

transcript.author: TAGLE
transcript.text: Yeah.

transcript.author: GOODEN
transcript.text: When have we ever been like, oh, my friends were like he ain't for me, and I was like, OK, I'm going to break up with him, right?

transcript.author: TAGLE
transcript.text: Yeah.

transcript.author: GOODEN
transcript.text: Like, that's not how it works. First of all, one on one. Do not stage an intervention. Do not go as a group and tell this girl that you don't like her fiancé. That is not going to work. She's not going to talk to you about the relationship anymore and you will not see him anymore.

transcript.author: LUSE
transcript.text: No.

transcript.author: TAGLE
transcript.text: You're not going to be close coworkers anymore.

transcript.author: LUSE
transcript.text: No. Yeah, I agree. As much as you could be 100% right. And that's the thing that'll hurt is, like, you could call it from jump.

transcript.author: TAGLE
transcript.text: Yeah.

transcript.author: LUSE
transcript.text: And you could be 100% right in the end - 100% right. But it is not something that is really always for you to point out as an outside party to the relationship, especially as a coworker. Now, as a super close sibling or a real homegirl, like, the bestie - right? - I generally think that you have maybe one time to say, hey, I just want to check in with you. Like, this is a big change. Like, how are you feeling? Do you really feel 100% sure this is the right person for you? And then if they say yes, you just go, all right. And then you got to...

transcript.author: TAGLE
transcript.text: OK, cool.

transcript.author: LUSE
transcript.text: ...Keep that mouth shut. Be a sounding board, be a friend, be supportive. And if you really care about this person as a friend, you don't want them to be in a situation where they're socially isolated with a partner who's really not nice to them. And, you know, maybe one point in the future, they will kind of see all these people around them. They're like, OK, well, this person makes me feel really good about myself. And I have so much fun with this person. And this one, like, is always picking up the phone and wants to talk to me. And this person always wants to go to, like, you know, the farmers market with me and try whatever new hobby I want to try. And they'll start to kind of see like, OK, well, if I feel great in all these other relationships where I'm really supported, maybe this person really isn't for me. It's a long game (laughter).

transcript.author: TAGLE
transcript.text: Yeah.

transcript.author: LUSE
transcript.text: And it's not necessarily for the weak. But, you know, if you really care about someone, then you really just want to have their back and be there as a friend.

transcript.author: TAGLE
transcript.text: (Grunting) Man, is that hard to do.

transcript.author: LUSE
transcript.text: Oh, it's hard, it's hard.

transcript.author: TAGLE
transcript.text: Before you go, we ask every guest of Dear LIFE KIT for their best piece of advice. We would love to hear yours.

transcript.author: LUSE
transcript.text: Block and delete, block and delete, block and delete.

transcript.author: GOODEN
transcript.text: (Laughter).

transcript.author: LUSE
transcript.text: Block and delete, block and delete, block and delete.

transcript.author: TAGLE
transcript.text: (Laughter).

transcript.author: LUSE
transcript.text: Situationship, ex, old hookup - if you want to move forward with your life, block and delete.

transcript.author: GOODEN
transcript.text: Yes.

transcript.author: LUSE
transcript.text: If you want to play in the past and fantasize and get your heart broken or keep it broken and waste time and be upset, do what you want to do. But if you want to move forward, block and delete.

transcript.author: GOODEN
transcript.text: Someone needed to hear that today.

transcript.author: LUSE
transcript.text: (Laughter).

transcript.author: GOODEN
transcript.text: Well, mine is a little different.

transcript.author: GOODEN
transcript.text: But it would be to learn to be a good friend to yourself, right? Like, we deal with so much. And the last thing we need is to be judging and criticizing ourselves constantly. So the more you can learn to be kind and compassionate and a good friend to yourself, I think the easier life is.

transcript.author: TAGLE
transcript.text: Adia and Brittany, thank you so much for your time. What a pleasure.

transcript.author: GOODEN
transcript.text: Thanks. This was fun.

transcript.author: LUSE
transcript.text: Thank you. This was so fun.

transcript.author: SEGARRA
transcript.text: That was LIFE KIT reporter Andee Tagle talking to Adia Gooden and Brittany Luse.

transcript.author: SEGARRA
transcript.text: And that's our show. Before we go, I do have a favor to ask you. If you have a second, would you leave LIFE KIT a five-star review? If LIFE KIT has ever helped you save a little money, make a healthier choice for yourself or it's just made you feel a little more seen, a five-star review is a great way to show your support. Thank you so much.

transcript.author: SEGARRA
transcript.text: This episode of LIFE KIT was produced by Sylvie Douglis. Our visuals editor is Beck Harlan, and our digital editor is Malaka Gharib. Meghan Keane is our senior supervising editor, and Beth Donovan is our executive producer. Our production team also includes Clare Marie Schneider and Margaret Cirino. Engineering support comes from Robert Rodriguez. I'm Marielle Segarra. Thanks for listening.

lines: You're listening to LIFE KIT...
...From NPR.

Hey. It's Marielle. Today, we're talking about how to deal with unwanted and unwarranted advice. Now, what's that old expression? Opinions are like adrenal glands - most of us have them, and we tend to think ours are the best. Which is to say, a lot of people have strong opinions about offering advice and opinions.
Oh, I love giving advice - unfortunately, unsolicited (laughter).

See what I mean? That was NPR's Brittany Luse, by the way. If you don't know her already, she's the host of It's Been A Minute, and every week, she does a deep dive into what's going on in our culture and helps listeners make sense of it. So it makes sense that she would have hot takes on things, and I would trust them.

But I do seek advice. I'm comfortable with not being an expert on something and not knowing something and going to somebody, like, to be like, can you help me with this, or what do you think I should do?
The thing is, there is a difference between someone attempting to be helpful and just straight-up judging or criticizing you.

I'm someone who's like, if you have wisdom, like, I'll learn from it. I'm happy to learn from it. But I do think it's different if you're like, no, I'm going along just fine, and I don't need your opinion that might be judgmental or, like, you're doing it wrong. Like, that advice is generally, like, nobody likes it.

That's clinical psychologist Adia Gooden. You might recognize her from our episodes on how to be a good loser or how to stop hurting your own feelings. She's all about embracing unconditional self-worth, a very useful skill to have in your back pocket when it comes to today's topic. On this edition of Dear LIFE KIT, the advice series from LIFE KIT reporter Andee Tagle, Brittany and Adia are going to give you advice about advice. They'll talk you through how to handle hurtful in-laws, overly curious onlookers and those family members that mean well but can't seem to quit with their lofty expectations.
OK, everybody, here's Question 1.

(Reading) Dear LIFE KIT, I'm struggling with my relationship with my future sister-in-law. She often says things that are hurtful or judgmental. One time, she told me, I love you, but you're too much of a self-absorbed person to own a dog. And this was over a meal that I had brought over for her. I've had pets in the past, raised my younger sister, taking care of my sick mom. I volunteer regularly. I don't live my life in a way that I think most would consider self-absorbed. She frequently comments on the decisions my fiancé, her brother and I make. Her comments make us second-guess our decisions and bring us down. In the past, when my fiancé has brought up an opposing opinion, she shuts him down and is adamant that she is right and he is wrong. So lately, I've begun avoiding her. How can I cope with the situation in a healthy way and have a healthy relationship with her? Signed, Feeling Frozen.

Well, I - you know, I think this is a situation of needing to recognize when someone's comments are not about you. I mean, many of the times when we interact with people, it's not - it's about them. It's not about us. And if you know for yourself that you are not selfish, that you are not self-absorbed, then you shouldn't let somebody's comment that you are derail you or override what you know to be true from your own experience, from how you've shown up. So I think the first thing is, how do you remember who you are and know who you are and be grounded in that? And then how do you determine and discern whose opinion you're going to take and whose opinion you're going to leave, right?

Like, if it's your wise grandma and she says, hey, it seems like you've been focusing on yourself a little too much and I want you to think about this, that might be very different than a sister-in-law who you don't really feel knows you, tends to make judgmental comments and is often making you feel bad, right? So she may be somebody who you're like, I'm going to leave your opinions over there because that's about you. It's really not about me. And then you may also need to set some boundaries. Like, hey, you know, it feels - I feel judged when you give me advice, so I'd prefer that not to be part of our relationship - or something, right? You may have a conversation at some point.
I echo that. I think that you can't control her. You can only control how you...
Right

...How and when you interact with her to a certain degree. And also, like, how you want to take on what she's saying to and about you. Another question that I have, though - where is your fiancé in all this? Like, at the end of the day...
Yeah, that was my question, too.

...That's his sister. And I think he could play maybe a bigger role in managing the - like, your relationship as a couple to her. It also sounds like maybe there's something going on where he's also taking on a lot as far as, like, her judgments of him and your guys' relationship. And that is also something to kind of, like, be aware of. Like, I remember my therapist at one point saying to me when I was talking about somebody that I was having a difficult issue with, and she said, you know, have you ever considered that this person may never change? Like, I want you to consider that...
Oof.

...And then think about how you want to conduct yourself going forward. And, yeah, I think that's not just something for you to do, but it sounds like it may be something for your fiancé to do as well.
Yeah. The one question that I had here is, as they're starting a new life, as they're starting to pull away from the sister, like, the optics of that can feel hard.

It seems like there might be space for them to distance without, like, totally cutting off. And I think if people question, you could also just - you can give people an opportunity to do something different. You can also say, hey, you know, every time you make these comments, it makes me feel judged and criticized, and I don't like it, and I'm hoping you could stop. And you can give them an opportunity to stop, and they can try. And if they do, great. And if they don't, they'll know exactly why there has been a shift.
Yeah.
Wow.

That makes me think of something that I say (laughter) as, like, a personal philosophy, which is, like, I'm going to give as much as I can lovingly and no more. 'Cause if you give more than what you can give lovingly, you're either going to - either leads to conflict, or it leads to you feeling shortchanged. Even if the other person is fine - right? - you're feeling terrible 'cause you feel like you overextended yourself and you got trampled on. And it just - it doesn't make anybody happy.
Wow. I love that.
That's a great philosophy.
Moving on to Question 2.

(Reading) Dear LIFE KIT, as a 6-foot-tall woman, I am often told by complete strangers variations of, wow, you're tall. It makes me feel uncomfortable and a bit like a spectacle. Some people say I should take it as a compliment, but I think it's rude for others to announce my most prominent feature to me with a tone of shock. I don't want to normalize the comments, but I don't want to embarrass the commenters either. How can I respond? Signed, More Than My Height.

More Than My Height, I appreciate the fact that you don't want to embarrass the people who are making these comments. But I just want to offer maybe not 100% embarrassment or even, like, 60% embarrassment, but maybe a nice little gritty 15% embarrassment where you can respond with, like, oh, my gosh, you are - you know, you're the first person to tell me that.

And just give them a little giggle. And then you kind of give them the opportunity to laugh with you, but they know, like, you're not playing. And then if they don't take the hint the first time, then you can just be direct and be like, is this the thing that's catching your attention so much that you have to say something right now? I just feel like if you can give someone sort of, like, one pass with a little joke to sort of let them know that you don't like it, and then after that, I think if you're really direct, then people will get it.

Yeah. I agree with Brittany. If people are continuing to sort of, like, cross this boundary, being more serious about it and being like, it's not cool. I'm not comfortable with it. Please stop. And I think, you know, if somebody - I agree. If somebody feels a little embarrassed or a little like, ooh, I shouldn't have done - it's like, that's true, and that's OK. You're not saying, you're an awful person that you would ever do - right? You're not doing that. But you're communicating, like, actually, what you did is problematic. Somebody else should...
Yeah.
...Feel a little taste of it so that they stop doing it.
Right.
That's good.
Yeah.
More questions from Dear LIFE KIT after this.
Moving on to Question No. 3.

(Reading) Dear LIFE KIT, I've been with my boyfriend for seven years, and I'm starting to feel pressure from family members and friends to settle down and have a wedding. We're planning on getting married, but neither of us are financially stable right now. We want to wait until we are in a more secure place to make wedding plans. How can I let people know that we're not in a hurry to get married without being rude? Signed...
I like this. Oh, go ahead. Sorry.

Yeah. Signed, Not In A Hurry, but also could be signed by everybody on Earth, for sure. We all have a version of this, I think. Single, partnered, married, not married, everyone loves to tell you that you are behind in your life. You are doing...
Yeah.
...It wrong. Brittany, thoughts?

I lived this. When you literally say - like, my husband and I, we got - he proposed after three years, which for some people is way too long. And then by the time we got married in 2022, we had been together about seven years (laughter). And everybody was asking, like, when are you getting married? And then as soon as we did that - think you got everyone off your back, and then everyone's like, OK, well, where are your kids? And, you know, at the end of the day, I think there's, like, two things happening. One thing is that people in your life probably see that you're very happy and are happy for your relationship and want to celebrate it and you and would love to see you feel the happiness that perhaps they get or have gotten from those celebrations that they've had of their own. So it can be coming from, like, a very loving place. And then...
Yes.

...There's also, like, another aspect of it, which is that people, I think, also sometimes feel a lot of anxiety if they feel like you are not hitting certain milestones in a timeline that's comfortable for them. And then they can take that anxiety and project it onto you. And, I mean, as you are bringing your lives together to get married, thinking back to that first question we discussed, now is a great time for you all to discuss and strategize how you're going to deal with feedback and input from other people. And what I said to people maybe is probably not as great as what Adia might say you should say to people. But I said to people, if you got $30,000 for me that you want to break off, like, if you want to deposit that into my...
(Laughter).

If you got tens of thousands of dollars that you want to give me, go right ahead. The other thing that I would say that was mildly gentler is, like, I don't have any updates to share right now, but when I do, you will be the first to know, which is what I say now when people ask me about having children. I say, oh, I don't have any updates right now. But when I do, you'll be the first to know.
That's so funny. That's exactly what I tell my parent when they ask about the second, when they ask about - so now my new benchmark is for a sibling.
(Laughter).
And I'm like, OK, are you paying for day care, or when are you moving in? Yeah, there's never an end to the benchmark. This is all very good. Adia, what do you have for us?

Yeah. I agree with what Brittany said. I think if people are coming from a loving place, just saying, like, you know, we're being really intentional and thoughtful about how we want to start our next chapter of our lives together. Weddings are expensive. We don't want to start our lives together in debt or going into debt. I think sort of touching on what Brittany mentioned about anxiety - right? - sometimes, you know, it could be anxiety of, like, you know, if you don't marry him right away, he going to leave. And so I think, again, sort of, like, it might be a time when you're like - either like, you know, like, thanks for your opinion, but we're going to do what's right for us, or, like, letting it roll off and just being like, OK. Like, I'm glad you - you know, that's not our situation. Like, we're not - I'm not worried he's going to leave me. I'm not worried this or that, the other's going to happen. I think making a joke about it can be good, too, because people are like, oh, oh. Oh, OK. Right? They'll usually back off.
I like that. My last question for you is, we all have those people in our lives that push harder, you know? There's a spectrum. So when that light touch doesn't work, what's level two?

What Brittany was saying. It's like, you know, please stop asking us. It's kind of frustrating and annoying when you keep asking us about our plans. We will be sure to let you know when we are going in that direction. But until then, I just ask that you respect our decision-making.

I agree. You just got to - you got to really push back. Or - I mean, I have never really had to do this, but, like, you could also - (laughter) like, if it gets to a certain point, you could just give them a complete, like, I already told you. I'm not discussing this. And then you just wait (laughter). Oh, my gosh. It unsettles people.
Yeah. 'Cause I think part of it is people want you to get into a negotiation with them.
Right.
Like, you feeling like, oh, I have to give all my reasons. And you, like what Brittany said, it's like, I already told you. It's like, I'm not engaging in this discussion. Like, our decision is made.
Right.
And because people want to be able to, I want to convince you, or why do you think? Well, no, our decision is made.
Yep.
And then not engaging.
Not negotiating your life plan.
So funny. I feel like so much of the advice we've given today is like, (laughter) don't take other people's advice.
Take our advice.
But not others'.
(Laughter) OK, our final question - Question No. 4.

Dear LIFE KIT, I've grown close with a group of coworkers lately. One friend in our group will be getting married later this year. Most of us have mixed feelings about our friend's upcoming marriage. She seems happy, but we disapprove of how her fiancé treats her. We think she deserves better. We've observed him in group settings. He's disengaged, hard to talk to and acts as if he's too good for the gathering. We consider that he might just be shy or awkward, but another coworker has noticed that our friend's fiancé withholds affection and bosses her around. A few of us talked it over and agreed that if we were in her situation, we would want someone to tell us. Should we tell her? Signed, Caring Coworkers.
I do not think it's true that they would want to know from a coworker if their coworker did not like their fiancé.
Right. Yeah. In what world? In what world?
You would not.
Ever.
You would not, right?
No, no.
Like, from one, from all?
Right.
You got to pull them into a conference room and be like...
She will cut you all off.

(Laughter) We've all had enough relationships with people we probably shouldn't have been with and seen friends in relationships with people that weren't great to know that it basically never works to be like, girl, he ain't for you.
Yeah. Yeah.
Yeah.
When have we listened to that?
Yeah.
When have we ever been like, oh, my friends were like he ain't for me, and I was like, OK, I'm going to break up with him, right?
Yeah.

Like, that's not how it works. First of all, one on one. Do not stage an intervention. Do not go as a group and tell this girl that you don't like her fiancé. That is not going to work. She's not going to talk to you about the relationship anymore and you will not see him anymore.
No.
You're not going to be close coworkers anymore.
No. Yeah, I agree. As much as you could be 100% right. And that's the thing that'll hurt is, like, you could call it from jump.
Yeah.

And you could be 100% right in the end - 100% right. But it is not something that is really always for you to point out as an outside party to the relationship, especially as a coworker. Now, as a super close sibling or a real homegirl, like, the bestie - right? - I generally think that you have maybe one time to say, hey, I just want to check in with you. Like, this is a big change. Like, how are you feeling? Do you really feel 100% sure this is the right person for you? And then if they say yes, you just go, all right. And then you got to...
OK, cool.

...Keep that mouth shut. Be a sounding board, be a friend, be supportive. And if you really care about this person as a friend, you don't want them to be in a situation where they're socially isolated with a partner who's really not nice to them. And, you know, maybe one point in the future, they will kind of see all these people around them. They're like, OK, well, this person makes me feel really good about myself. And I have so much fun with this person. And this one, like, is always picking up the phone and wants to talk to me. And this person always wants to go to, like, you know, the farmers market with me and try whatever new hobby I want to try. And they'll start to kind of see like, OK, well, if I feel great in all these other relationships where I'm really supported, maybe this person really isn't for me. It's a long game (laughter).
Yeah.
And it's not necessarily for the weak. But, you know, if you really care about someone, then you really just want to have their back and be there as a friend.
(Grunting) Man, is that hard to do.
Oh, it's hard, it's hard.
Before you go, we ask every guest of Dear LIFE KIT for their best piece of advice. We would love to hear yours.
Block and delete, block and delete, block and delete.
(Laughter).
Block and delete, block and delete, block and delete.
(Laughter).
Situationship, ex, old hookup - if you want to move forward with your life, block and delete.
Yes.
If you want to play in the past and fantasize and get your heart broken or keep it broken and waste time and be upset, do what you want to do. But if you want to move forward, block and delete.
Someone needed to hear that today.
(Laughter).
Well, mine is a little different.

But it would be to learn to be a good friend to yourself, right? Like, we deal with so much. And the last thing we need is to be judging and criticizing ourselves constantly. So the more you can learn to be kind and compassionate and a good friend to yourself, I think the easier life is.
Adia and Brittany, thank you so much for your time. What a pleasure.
Thanks. This was fun.
Thank you. This was so fun.
That was LIFE KIT reporter Andee Tagle talking to Adia Gooden and Brittany Luse.

And that's our show. Before we go, I do have a favor to ask you. If you have a second, would you leave LIFE KIT a five-star review? If LIFE KIT has ever helped you save a little money, make a healthier choice for yourself or it's just made you feel a little more seen, a five-star review is a great way to show your support. Thank you so much.
